otin{W}$ŚíĆ$S(\mathscr{A})Q(\mathscr{A})	extbf{u}\in{W}$ŚźĆśó∂śąźÁęč„ÄāšĹÜśėĮ P_{	ext{cond }W,\mathscr{A},	extbf{u+v}}(\mathscr{A})(	extbf{u}+	extbf{v})=S(\mathscr{A})Q(\mathscr{A})	extbf{v}+Q(\mathscr{A})S(\mathscr{A})	extbf{u}\in{W}ŤŅôŚĺóŚąįšļÜÁüõÁõĺ„ÄāŚõ†ś≠§$R=1$„ÄāŚľēÁźÜ1ŚĺóŤĮĀ„Äā ÔľąŚľēÁźÜ2ÔľČŤģĺ$P(\lambda)$śėĮšłÄšł™Śú®$\mathbb{F}$šłäšłćŚŹĮÁļ¶ÁöĄŚ§öť°ĻŚľŹÔľĆŤģĺśúČś≠£śēīśēį$m$šĹŅŚĺó$(P(\lambda))^m|P_{	ext{cond }W,\mathscr{A}(\lambda)}$ÔľĆťā£šĻąŚ≠ėŚú®$	extbf{v}\in{V}$šĹŅŚĺó$P_{	ext{cond },W,\mathscr{A},	extbf{v}}(\lambda)=(P(\lambda))^m$ ÁĒĪśĚ°šĽ∂ŚŹĮšĽ•Ťģĺ P_{	ext{cond }W,\mathscr{A}}(\lambda)=(P(\lambda))^mQ(\lambda)ś≥®śĄŹŚąįšłÄŚģöŚ≠ėŚú®$	extbf{v}_0$šĹŅŚĺó (P(\mathscr{A}))^{m-1}Q(\mathscr{A})	extbf{v}_0
otin{W}Śź¶ŚąôÔľĆśúČ$(P(\lambda))^{m-1}Q(\lambda)\in{I(W,\mathscr{A})}$ÔľĆŤÄĆś¨°śēįšĹéšļé$P_{	ext{cond }W,\mathscr{A}}(\lambda)$„ÄāŚ∑≤Áü•$P_{	ext{cond }W,\mathscr{A}}(\lambda)$śėĮ$I(W,\mathscr{A})$ťáĆś¨°śēįśúÄšĹéÁöĄÔľĆśČÄšĽ•ŤŅôŚĺóŚąįšļÜÁüõÁõĺ. ŚŹ¶šłÄśĖĻťĚĘÔľĆ$(P(\mathscr{A}))^mQ(\mathscr{A})	extbf{v}_0=P_{	ext{cond }W,\mathscr{A}}(\mathscr{A})\in{W}$„ÄāšĽ§$	extbf{u}=Q(\mathscr{A})	extbf{v}_0$ÔľĆťā£šĻąŚįĪśúČ (P(\mathscr{A}))^m	extbf{u}\in{W} Ś∑≤ÁĽŹť™ĆŤĮĀÔľĆŤŅôťáĆÁöĄ$m$šłćŤÉĹŚÜćšĹéÔľĆŚźĆśó∂šĻüšłćťöĺť™ĆŤĮĀ$P^m$šłļť¶ĖšłÄŚ§öť°ĻŚľŹ„ÄāśČÄšĽ•śúČ P_{	ext{cond },W,\mathscr{A},	extbf{u}}(\lambda)=(P(\lambda))^mŚľēÁźÜ2ŚĺóŤĮĀ śúÄŚźéśĚ•ŤÄÉŤôĎŚģöÁźÜ1„Äāť¶ĖŚÖąÔľĆŚüü$\mathbb{F}$šłäšĽĽśĄŹťĚěŚłłśēįŚ§öť°ĻŚľŹ$f$ťÉĹŚŹĮšĽ•ŚĒĮšłÄŚąÜŤß£šłļšłćŚŹĮÁļ¶Śõ†Ś≠źÔľĆŚć≥ f=\prod_{i=1}^{m}p_iŚÖ∂šł≠$p_i$śėĮšłćŚŹĮÁļ¶ÁöĄÔľĆšłĒŤŅôÁßćšĻėÁßĮśėĮŚĒĮšłÄÁöĄ(šłćŤÄÉŤôĎŚłłśēįť°ĻŚíĆś¨°ŚļŹ). ťā£šĻą$P_{	ext{cond },W,\mathscr{A}}(\lambda)$ŚįĪŚŹĮšĽ•ŤŅõŤ°ĆŚąÜŤß£(Ś¶āśěúŚĮľŚ≠źśėĮŚłłśēįÔľĆÁĽďŤģļśėĮśėĺÁĄ∂ÁöĄ)ÔľĆŚźąŚĻ∂ÁõłŚźĆŚõ†Ś≠źšĻčŚźéśúČ P_{	ext{cond },W,\mathscr{A}}(\lambda)=(P_1(\lambda))^{r_1}(P_2(\lambda))^{r_2}\cdots(P_n(\lambda))^{r_n}ťā£šĻąś†ĻśćģŚľēÁźÜ2ŚįĪśúČ$	extbf{v}_1,	extbf{v}_2,\cdots,	extbf{v}_n\in{V}$šĹŅŚĺó P_{	ext{cond }W,\mathscr{A}}(\lambda)=\prod_{i=1}^{n}P_{	ext{cond }W,\mathscr{A},	extbf{v}_i}(\lambda)ŚŹąś†ĻśćģŚľēÁźÜ1ÔľĆšĽ§$	extbf{v}_1+	extbf{v}_2+\cdots+	extbf{v}_n=	extbf{v}$ÔľĆŚįĪŤÉĹŚĺóŚąį P_{	ext{cond }W,\mathscr{A}}(\lambda)=P_{	ext{cond }W,\mathscr{A},	extbf{v}}(\lambda)ŚĺóŤĮĀ„Äā

posts.title: ÁļŅśÄßÁ©ļťóīÁöĄŚĺ™ÁéĮŚąÜŤß£
posts.text:
ŤĮīŚú®ŚČćťĚĘŤŅôÁĮáŚćöŚģĘšł≠šĽÖšĽÖŚľēŚÖ•šļÜŚĺ™ÁéĮŚ≠źÁ©ļťóīÁöĄś¶āŚŅĶÔľĆťĀóÁēôšļÜŚĺąŚ§öťóģťĘėś≤°ŚĺóŚąįŤß£ŚÜ≥„ÄāŤŅôšļõťóģťĘėšľöŚú®ŤŅôÁĮáŚćöŚģĘšł≠ŚĺóŚąįŤß£ŚÜ≥„ÄāšłÄšł™Áü©ťėĶÁöĄśúČÁźÜś†áŚáÜŚĹĘŤôĹÁĄ∂šłćŤÉĹŚĺóŚąįšłéÁČĻŚĺĀŚÄľ„ÄĀÁČĻŚĺĀŚźĎťáŹśúČŚÖ≥ÁöĄŚÜÖŚģĻÔľĆšĹÜśėĮŚú®ŚąÜŤß£śúüťóīŚŹ™ś∂ČŚŹäŚąįšļÜŚä†ś≥ēŚíĆšĻėś≥ēÔľĆšłćšľöŚáļÁéįśČ©ŚÖÖśēįŚüüÁöĄÁéįŤĪ°„ÄāŚŹĮšĽ•ŤĮīÔľĆśúČÁźÜś†áŚáÜŚĹĘŤÉĹŚĺóŚąįšłÄÁĪĽÁõłšľľÁü©ťėĶśõīśúīÁī†ÁöĄšŅ°śĀĮ. Śĺ™ÁéĮŚąÜŤß£ Ťģĺ$V$śėĮ$\mathbb{F}$šłäÁöĄ$n$ÁĽīÁļŅśÄßÁ©ļťóīÔľĆ$\mathscr{A}$śėĮ$V$ÁöĄÁļŅśÄßŚŹėśćĘÔľĆŚąô V= igoplus_{i=1}^r\mathbb{F} ig[\mathscr{A} ig]\alpha_i ŚÖ∂šł≠ÔľĆ$\mathbb{F} [\mathscr{A}] \alpha_i
eq\{0\}$šłļ$\alpha_i\in{V}$ÁĒüśąźÁöĄŚĺ™ÁéĮŚ≠źÁ©ļťóī. ś≥®śĄŹÔľĆŤŅôťáĆÁöĄÁõīŚíĆŚĎäŤĮČśąĎšĽ¨ÔľĆ$\alpha_1,\alpha_2,\cdots,\alpha_r$ÁļŅśÄßśó†ŚÖ≥„Äā ŤŅôťáĆśąĎšĽ¨šľöÁĒ®ŚąįŤŅôÁĮáŚćöŚģĘťáĆśŹźŚąįÁöĄŚĮľŚ≠ź„ÄāŚÖ∂ŚģěÔľĆŤŅôšłÄŤäāšĻüŚŹĮšĽ•ÁúčśąźŚĮľŚ≠źśÄßŤī®ÁöĄŤ°•ŚÖÖ„ÄāśąĎšĽ¨šľöÁĒ®ŚąįšłčťĚĘšłÄšł™Śüļśú¨šļčŚģěÔľöŤģĺ$U \subset W \subset V$ÔľĆŚąôśúČ P_{	ext{cond }W,\mathscr{A}}(\lambda)|P_{	ext{cond }U,\mathscr{A}}(\lambda).ŤŅôśėĮŚõ†šłļÔľĆŚĮĻšļéšĽĽśĄŹÁöĄ$\alpha \in V$ÔľĆśąĎšĽ¨śúČ$P_{	ext{cond }U,\mathscr{A}}(\lambda)\alpha \in U \subset W$ÔľĆŚõ†ś≠§$P_{	ext{cond }U,\mathscr{A}}(\lambda) \in I(W,\mathscr{A})$. šĽéś≠§ŤŅėŤÉĹŚĺóŚąįÁöĄšłÄšł™ÁĽďŤģļśėĮ$I(U,\mathscr{A}) \subset I(W,\mathscr{A})$. Śú®Ś≠¶šĻ†šļÜšļ§śćĘšĽ£śēįÔľĆÁČĻŚąęśėĮKrullÁĽīŚļ¶ÁõłŚÖ≥ÁöĄŚÜÖŚģĻÔľĆŚįĪšľöŚĮĻŤŅôťáĆśúČšłćšłÄś†∑ÁöĄÁźÜŤß£„ÄāšĹÜśėĮŤŅôŚ∑≤ÁĽŹŤŅúŤŅúŤ∂ÖŚáļŤŅôÁĮáŚćöŚģĘÁöĄŤĆÉŚõī„Äā Śĺ™ÁéĮŚąÜŤß£ÁöĄŤĮĀśėéŚ¶āśěú$V$śú¨ŤļęśėĮšłÄšł™ÁõłŚĮĻšļé$\mathscr{A}$ÁöĄŚĺ™ÁéĮÁ©ļťóīÔľĆťā£šĻąŤĮĀśėéÁĽďśĚü„ÄāÁéįŚú®Ťģ®ŤģļŚŹ¶šłÄÁßćśÉÖŚÜĶ. Ťģ®ŤģļŚĮľŚ≠źśó∂śąĎšĽ¨šļÜŤß£ŚąįÔľĆŚ≠ėŚú®$\alpha_1\in{V}$šĹŅŚĺó P_{	ext{min }\mathscr{A}}(\lambda)=P_{	ext{min }\mathscr{A},\alpha_1}(\lambda)Ťģĺ$W_1=\mathbb{F}[\mathscr{A}]\alpha_1$ÔľĆŚĻ∂šłļśĖĻšĺŅŤĶ∑ŤßĀŤģĺ$W_0=0$. ŚŹąŚŹĮšĽ•ťÄČŚŹĖ$ eta_2 \in V \setminus W_1$šĹŅŚĺó R_2(\lambda)=P_{	ext{cond }W_1,\mathscr{A}}(\lambda)=P_{	ext{cond }W_1,\mathscr{A}, eta_2}(\lambda)śąĎšĽ¨Ťá™ÁĄ∂ŚłĆśúõÔľĆ$R_2(\lambda)$ÁöĄ‚ÄúŚúįšĹć‚ÄĚŚíĆ$P_{	ext{min }\mathscr{A},\alpha_1}(\lambda)$ ÁĪĽšľľ„ÄāŚĮĻś≠§ÔľĆśąĎšĽ¨śĖ≠Ť®ÄÔľĆŚŹĮšĽ•ŤįÉśēī$ eta_2$Ťá≥śüź$\alpha_2$ÔľĆšĹŅŚĺó$R_2(\lambda)=P_{	ext{min }\mathscr{A},\alpha_2}(\lambda)$. ŤĮĀśėéś≠§śĖ≠Ť®ÄŚźéÔľĆśąĎšĽ¨ŚŹĮšĽ•ÁĒ®ÁĪĽšľľÁöĄŚäěś≥ēÁĽßÁĽ≠ŚĮĽśČĺŚźéťĚĘÁöĄŚ§öť°ĻŚľŹÔľĆśúÄÁĽąŚĺóŚąįÁĽďŤģļ„Äā ť¶ĖŚÖąÔľĆ$R_2(\mathscr{A}) eta_2=Q_1(\mathscr{A})\alpha_1$. śąĎšĽ¨ť¶ĖŚÖąśĄüŚÖīŤ∂£ÁöĄśėĮ$Q_1$ŚíĆ$R_2$ÁöĄŚÖ≥Á≥Ľ„ÄāŚĮĻś≠§ÔľĆŤģĺ Q_1(\lambda)=R_2(\lambda)h_1(\lambda)+r_1(\lambda),\quad \deg r_1 < \deg R_2.ŚąôÁęčŚąĽŚĺóŚąį$R_2(\mathscr{A}) eta_2=R_2(\mathscr{A})h_1(\mathscr{A})\alpha_1+r_1(\mathscr{A})\alpha_1$. šĽ§ \alpha_2= eta_2-h_1(\mathscr{A})\alpha_1 \in  eta+W_1.Śąô R_2(\mathscr{A})\alpha_2=R_2(\mathscr{A})h_1(\mathscr{A})\alpha_1+r_1(\mathscr{A})\alpha_1-R_2(\mathscr{A})h_1(\mathscr{A})\alpha_1=r_1(\mathscr{A})\alpha_1 \in W_1.ŤŅôšĻüŤĮīśėéšļÜ$R_2(\lambda)=P_{	ext{cond }W_1,\mathscr{A}, eta_2}(\lambda)=P_{	ext{ cond }W_1,\mathscr{A},\alpha_2 }(\lambda)$. ŤŅôśėĮÁĒĪŚĮľŚ≠źÁöĄŚģöšĻČŚÜ≥ŚģöÁöĄ ÁĒĪšļé$W_0 \subset W_1$, śąĎšĽ¨ŚŹąśúČ P_{	ext{cond }W_1,\mathscr{A},\alpha_2}(\lambda)|P_{	ext{cond }W_0,\mathscr{A},\alpha_2}(\lambda).ś≥®śĄŹŚąį$P_{	ext{cond }W_0,\mathscr{A},\alpha_2}(\lambda)=P_{	ext{min }\mathscr{A},\alpha_2}(\lambda)$. šĽéŤÄĆśąĎšĽ¨ŚŹĮšĽ•śēīÁźÜśąź R_2(\lambda)|P_{	ext{min }\mathscr{A},\alpha_2}(\lambda).ŚĮĻšļéšłäŚľŹśąĎšĽ¨ŚŹĮšĽ•ŚÜôśąź P_{	ext{min }\mathscr{A},\alpha_2}(\lambda)=g(\lambda)R_2(\lambda).šĽéś≠§ŚŹĮšĽ•ŚĺóŚąį g(\mathscr{A})P_{	ext{cond }W_0,\mathscr{A},\alpha_2}(\mathscr{A})\alpha_2=g(\mathscr{A})r_1(\mathscr{A})\alpha_1=0.Ťč•$r_1 
e 0$, Śąô$P_{	ext{min }\mathscr{A},\alpha_1}(\lambda)|[g(\lambda)r_1(\lambda)]$. šĽéŤÄĆ  egin{aligned} \deg g(\lambda)r_1(\lambda) &\ge \deg P_{	ext{min }\mathscr{A},\alpha_1}(\lambda) \ &= \deg P_{	ext{min }\mathscr{A}}(\lambda) \ &\ge \deg P_{	ext{min }\mathscr{A},\alpha_2}(\lambda) \ &= \deg g(\lambda) R_2(\lambda). \end{aligned} Śõ†ś≠§$\deg r_1(\lambda) \ge \deg R_2(\lambda)$ÔľĆŤŅôšłé$r_1(\lambda)$ÁöĄŚģöšĻČÁõłÁüõÁõĺÔľĆśēÖśąĎšĽ¨šłÄŚģöśúČ$r_1(\lambda)=0$. Śõ†ś≠§$R_2(\mathscr{A})\alpha_2=0$ÔľĆšĽéŤÄĆ$P_{	ext{min }\mathscr{A},\alpha_2}(\lambda)|R_2(\lambda)$ÔľĆŤŅôŤĮīśėé$R_2(\lambda)=P_{	ext{min }\mathscr{A},\alpha_2}(\lambda)$. śąĎšĽ¨ÁöĄśĖ≠Ť®ÄŚĺóŚąįšļÜŤĮĀśėé. šĽ§$W_2=\mathbb{F}[\mathscr{A}]\alpha_1+\mathbb{F}[\mathscr{A}]\alpha_2$„ÄāÁéįŚú®ŤĮĀśėéŚ≠źÁ©ļťóīÁöĄŚíĆšłļÁõīŚíĆ„Äā ŚĮĻšļé$\alpha\in W_1\cap\mathbb{F}[\mathscr{A}]\alpha_2$ÔľĆŚŹĮšĽ•Ťģĺ \alpha=P(\mathscr{A})\alpha_2 ŤÄĆ P_{	ext{cond }W_1,\mathscr{A},\alpha_2}(\lambda)|P(\lambda)śēÖ$P(\lambda)$śėĮšłÄšł™ťõ∂ŚĆĖŚ§öť°ĻŚľŹÔľĆŚõ†ś≠§$\alpha=0$„ÄāŤŅôŤĮīśėéÔľĆŚ≠źÁ©ļťóīÁöĄŚíĆšłļÁõīŚíĆ„ÄāŚõ†ś≠§ŚĺóŚąįšļÜŚ≠źÁ©ļťóīÁöĄÁõīŚíĆ W_2=\mathbb{F}[\mathscr{A}]\alpha_1\oplus\mathbb{F}[\mathscr{A}]\alpha_2Ś¶āśěú$W_2
eq{V}$ÔľĆťā£šĻąŚŹąŚŹĖ$ eta_3$šĹŅŚĺó R_3(\lambda)=P_{	ext{cond }W_2,\mathscr{A}}(\lambda)=P_{	ext{cond }W_2,\mathscr{A}, eta_3}(\lambda)śĆČÁÖßšłäťĚĘÁöĄŚäěś≥ēÔľĆśąĎšĽ¨ŚŹĮšĽ•ŚįÜ$ eta_3$ŤįÉśēīšłļśąĎšĽ¨śÉ≥Ť¶ĀÁöĄ$\alpha_3$ÔľĆŤŅôťáĆťúÄŤ¶Āś≥®śĄŹÁöĄśėĮÔľĆśąĎšĽ¨ťúÄŤ¶ĀŚÜô  egin{aligned} R_3(\mathscr{A}) eta_3 &=Q_1(\mathscr{A})\alpha_1+Q_2(\mathscr{A})\alpha_2 \ &=(R_2(\mathscr{A})h_1(\mathscr{A})\alpha_1+R_3(\mathscr{A})h_2(\mathscr{A})\alpha_2)+r_1(\mathscr{A})\alpha_1+r_2(\mathscr{A})\alpha_2 \end{aligned}šĽéŤÄĆśĆČÁÖßŚźĆś†∑ÁöĄŚäěś≥ēŤĮĀśėé$r_1=r_2=0$. ŤįÉśēīŤá≥$\alpha_1$ŚźéÔľĆśąĎšĽ¨ŚĺóŚąįŚ≠źÁ©ļťóī W_3=\mathbb{F}[\mathscr{A}]\alpha_1\oplus\mathbb{F}[\mathscr{A}]\alpha_2\oplus\mathbb{F}[\mathscr{A}]\alpha_3.Ś¶āś≠§ÁĽßÁĽ≠šłčŚéĽÔľĆŚ¶āśěú$W_k
eq{V}$ÔľĆŚąôŚŹĮÁĽßÁĽ≠ťÄČśč©$\alpha_{k+1}$Á≠ČÔľĆÁõīŚąįŚĺóŚąį V=\mathbb{F}[\mathscr{A}]\alpha_1\oplus\mathbb{F}[\mathscr{A}]\alpha_2\oplus\cdots\oplus\mathbb{F}[\mathscr{A}]\alpha_rś≠§Ść≥Śĺ™ÁéĮŚąÜŤß£„ÄāŚĮľŚ≠źÁöĄśÄßŤī®šŅĚŤĮĀŤŅôšł™ŚźĎťáŹÁöĄťÄČśč©ŚŹĮšĽ•ŤŅõŤ°ĆšłčŚéĽ„Äā$V$śėĮśúČťôźÁĽīÁöĄÔľĆśČÄšĽ•ŤŅôšł™ŤŅáÁ®čśÄĽšľöÁĽďśĚü„Äā šłćŚŹėŚõ†Ś≠źšłéśěĀŚįŹŚ§öť°ĻŚľŹŤŅôšłÄŤäāśąĎšĽ¨śĚ•śéĘŤģ®šłÄšłčšłäšłÄŤäāšł≠ŚĺóŚąįÁöĄŚźĄšł™Ťč•ŚĻ≤Ś§öť°ĻŚľŹ„ÄāŤģĺ$\alpha_k$ŚĮĻŚļĒÁöĄŚ§öť°ĻŚľŹšłļ$m_k(\lambda)$„Äāťā£šĻąÔľĆś†ĻśćģŚĮľŚ≠źÁöĄŚĒĮšłÄśÄßÔľĆŤŅôšļõŚ§öť°ĻŚľŹťÉĹśėĮŚĒĮšłÄÁöĄ(ŤĘę$\mathscr{A}$ŚĒĮšłÄŚÜ≥Śģö)ÔľĆŤÄĆšłĒŚ∑≤ÁĽŹŚĺóŚąįšļÜ$m_k|m_{k-1}$ŤŅôšłÄŚÖ≥Á≥Ľ„ÄāšĻüŚįĪśėĮŤĮīÔľĆŚĮĻšļéśĆáŚģöÁöĄśúČťôźÁĽīŚļ¶ÁļŅśÄßÁ©ļťóī$V$ŚíĆÁļŅśÄßŚŹėśćĘ$\mathscr{A}$ÔľĆ(Śú®ť°ļŚļŹšłä)ŚĒĮšłÄŚÜ≥ŚģöšļÜšłÄÁĽĄŚ§öť°ĻŚľŹ$m_1,m_2,\cdots,m_r$„ÄāŤŅôÁĽĄŚ§öť°ĻŚľŹÁßįšłļ$\mathscr{A}$ÁöĄšłćŚŹėŚõ†Ś≠ź„Äā šłäťĚĘÁöĄŤĮĀśėéšł≠Ś∑≤ÁĽŹŤß£ťáäÔľĆ$m_k$śėĮ$\alpha_k$ÁöĄśúÄŚįŹťõ∂ŚĆĖŚ≠ź„Äāťā£šĻąŚĮĻšļéśēīšł™Śĺ™ÁéĮŚ≠źÁ©ļťóī$\mathbb{F}[\mathscr{A}]\alpha_k$ŚŹąśėĮśÄéś†∑? ŚĮĻšļé$\alpha\in\mathbb{F}[\mathscr{A}]\alpha_k$ÔľĆŚŹĮšĽ•Ťģĺ \alpha = P(\mathscr{A})\alpha_kťā£šĻą m_k(\mathscr{A})\alpha=m_k(\mathscr{A})P(\mathscr{A})\alpha_k=0šĻüŚįĪśėĮŤĮīÔľĆ$m_k$šĻüśėĮ$\mathscr{A}$ťôźŚą∂Śú®$\mathbb{F}[\mathscr{A}]\alpha_k$ÁöĄśěĀŚįŹŚ§öť°ĻŚľŹ(ś¨°śēįšłćŤÉĹŚÜćšĹéÔľĆŚź¶ŚąôŚįĪšłćŚÜćśėĮ$\alpha_k$ÁöĄťõ∂ŚĆĖŚ≠ź). śúČÁźÜś†áŚáÜŚĹĘšłéÁČĻŚĺĀŚ§öť°ĻŚľŹ ŚĮĻŚüü$\mathbb{F}$šłäšĽĽšłÄśĖĻťėĶ$A$ÔľĆŚ≠ėŚú®Śüü$\mathbb{F}$šłäÁöĄŚŹĮťÄÜśĖĻťėĶ$P$šĹŅŚĺó B=P^{-1}AP=	ext{diag}(C(m_1),\cdots,C(m_r)) ŚÖ∂šł≠ÔľĆ$C(m_k)$śėĮ$m_k$ÁöĄŚŹčťėĶÔľĆ$m_k$śėĮÁĒĪ$A$ŚÜ≥ŚģöÁöĄÁļŅśÄßŚŹėśćĘ$x\mapsto{Ax}$ŚÜ≥ŚģöÁöĄÁļŅśÄßŚŹėśćĘÁöĄšłćŚŹėŚõ†Ś≠ź. śĖĻťėĶ$B$Ść≥šłļśĖĻťėĶ$A$ÁöĄśúČÁźÜś†áŚáÜŚĹĘ. Śú®ŤŅôÁĮáŚćöŚģĘťáĆśąĎšĽ¨śĆáŚáļÔľĆŚĮĻšļéŚĺ™ÁéĮŚ≠źÁ©ļťóī$W=\mathbb{F}[\mathscr{A}]\alpha$ÔľĆ$\mathscr{A}$ťôźŚą∂Śú®$W$šłäÁöĄÁü©ťėĶ$A_W=C(m)$„ÄāŤŅôśó∂śąĎšĽ¨ťÄČŚŹĖšļÜšłÄÁĽĄ$W$šłäÁöĄŚĺ™ÁéĮŚüļ„ÄāÁéįŚú®ÔľĆśąĎšĽ¨Ś∑≤ÁĽŹśääšłÄšł™ÁļŅśÄßÁ©ļťóīŚąÜŤß£śąźŤč•ŚĻ≤šł™Śĺ™ÁéĮŚ≠źÁ©ļťóīÁöĄÁõīŚíĆÔľĆŚ¶āśěúśąĎšĽ¨ťÄČśč©śĮŹšł™Ś≠źÁ©ļťóīÁöĄšłÄÁĽĄŚĺ™ÁéĮŚüļÔľĆŚć≥ŚĺóŚąįśúČÁźÜś†áŚáÜŚĹĘ. Ťģĺ$k_i=	ext{deg }m_i$ÔľĆŚąôŚú®ŚĮĻšļéŚüļ \alpha_1,\mathscr{A}\alpha_1,\cdots,\mathscr{A}^{k_1-1}\alpha_1,\cdots,\alpha_r,\mathscr{A}\alpha_r,\cdots,\mathscr{A}^{k_r-1}\alpha_rŤÄĆŤ®ÄÔľĆ$\mathscr{A}$ÁöĄśĖĻťėĶŤ°®Á§ļŚć≥šłļ A=	ext{diag}(C(m_1),\cdots,C(m_r))ŤŅôťáĆÁöĄ$P$Ść≥šłļÁĒĪŤŅôÁĽĄŚüļśěĄśąźÁöĄÁü©ťėĶ„ÄāŤģ°ÁģóÁČĻŚĺĀŚ§öť°ĻŚľŹšłćťöĺŚŹĎÁéįśúČ f=m_1m_2\cdots m_ršłÄšļõŤ°•ŚÖÖś†Ļśćģ$f$ŚíĆšłćŚŹėŚõ†Ś≠źÁöĄŚÖ≥Á≥ĽÔľĆšłćťöĺŚĺóŚąįšłČśĚ°ÁĽďŤģļ: $V$śėĮŚĺ™ÁéĮÁ©ļťóīŚĹďšłĒšĽÖŚĹď$f=m$„ÄāšĻüŚįĪśėĮŤĮīÔľĆśěĀŚįŹŚ§öť°ĻŚľŹŚíĆÁČĻŚĺĀŚ§öť°ĻŚľŹÁõłÁ≠Č„Äā $m|f$šłĒ$m$ŚíĆ$f$śúČÁõłŚźĆÁöĄšłćŚŹĮÁļ¶Śõ†Ś≠ź(ŚįĹÁģ°ś¨°śēįŚŹĮŤÉĹšłćŚźĆ)„ÄāšĻüŚįĪśėĮŤĮīÔľĆŚĮĻšļé$f$ŚŹĮšĽ•ŚĒĮšłÄŚąÜŤß£śąźšłćŚŹĮÁļ¶Śõ†Ś≠źf=p_1^{d_1}\cdots p_s^{d_s} ťā£šĻąšłÄŚģöśúČś≠£śēīśēį$r_1,\cdots,r_s$šĹŅŚĺó m=p_1^{r_1}\cdots p_s^{r_s}ŚÖ∂šł≠$0<r_i\leq d_i$„Äā Śĺ™ÁéĮŚąÜŤß£šĻüśąźšļÜCayley-HamiltonÁöĄŤĮĀśėé„ÄāŚõ†šłļ$m|f$ÔľĆśČÄšĽ•ÁČĻŚĺĀŚ§öť°ĻŚľŹ$f$šłÄŚģöśėĮťõ∂ŚĆĖŚ§öť°ĻŚľŹ„Äā Śĺ™ÁéĮŚąÜŤß£ÁöĄŚģěšĺč śĪāÁü©ťėĶ A= egin{pmatrix} 0&-1&2&0 \ -1&0&-2&0 \ 0&0&-5&0 \ 1&1&-2&1 \end{pmatrix} ÁöĄśúČÁźÜś†áŚáÜŚĹĘÔľĆŚĮĻ$\mathbb{R}^4$ŤŅõŤ°ĆŚĺ™ÁéĮŚąÜŤß£ ŤŅôśėĮÁļŅśÄßŚŹėśćĘ $\mathscr{A}:x\mapsto{Ax}$Śú®$\varepsilon_1,\varepsilon_2,\varepsilon_3,\varepsilon_4$šłčÁöĄśĖĻťėĶŤ°®Á§ļ. ŚŹĮšĽ•ÁģóŚáļ$f$ÁöĄÁČĻŚĺĀŚ§öť°ĻŚľŹŚíĆśěĀŚįŹŚ§öť°ĻŚľŹšłļ f(\lambda)=(\lambda-1)^2(\lambda+1)(\lambda+5) \ m(\lambda)=(\lambda-1)(\lambda+1)(\lambda+5)ŤŅôśó∂ŚÖ∂ŚģěŚ∑≤ÁĽŹŚģĆśąźšļÜŚąÜŤß£„ÄāŚõ†šłļšłćŚŹėŚõ†Ś≠źŚ∑≤ÁĽŹŤĘęÁ°ģŚģöÔľĆšĻüŚįĪśėĮŤĮīÔľĆ m_1=\lambda^3+5\lambda^2-\lambda-5 \ m_2=\lambda-1ťā£šĻąśúČÁźÜś†áŚáÜŚĹĘŚįĪśúČ B=\left( egin{array}{c c c|c} 0&0&	extbf{5}&0 \ 1&0&	extbf{1}&0 \ 0&1&	extbf{-5}&0 \ \hline 0&0&0&	extbf{1} \end{array}ight)śé•šłčśĚ•śąĎšĽ¨Ť¶ĀśĪāŚŹĮťÄÜÁü©ťėĶ$P$„Äāť¶ĖŚÖąŤģ®ŤģļŚ≠źÁ©ļťóī$\mathbb{R}[\mathscr{A}]\alpha_2$„ÄāŚĮĻšļé$m_2=\lambda-1$ÔľĆŚŹ™ťúÄŤ¶ĀŚŹĖ$A$ŚĪěšļéÁČĻŚĺĀŚÄľ$1$ÁöĄÁČĻŚĺĀŚźĎťáŹŚć≥ŚŹĮ„ÄāšłćŚ¶®Áõīśé•ŚŹĖ$\alpha_2=(0,0,0,1)^T$. ŚĮĻšļéŚŹ¶šłÄšł™Śĺ™ÁéĮÁ©ļťóīÁöĄÁĒüśąźŚÖÉ$\alpha_1$ÔľĆŚŹ™ťúÄŤ¶ĀšŅĚŤĮĀ$\alpha_1,\mathscr{A}\alpha_1,\mathscr{A}^2\alpha_1,\alpha_2$ÁļŅśÄßśó†ŚÖ≥(Áß©šłļ$4$)ÔľĆšĽéŤÄĆśěĄśąźŚĺ™ÁéĮŚüļÔľĆšłćŚ¶®ŚŹĖ$\alpha_1=(1,1,1,1)^T$„Äāś≠§śó∂$A\alpha_1=(1,-3,-5,1)^T$ÔľĆ$A^2\alpha_1=(-7,9,25,9)^T$„Äāś≠§śó∂ŚįĪŚĺóŚąįÁü©ťėĶ P= egin{pmatrix} 1&1&-7&0 \ 1&-3&9&0 \ 1&-5&25&0 \ 1&1&9&1 \end{pmatrix}šłĒ \mathbb{R}^4=\mathbb{R}[\mathscr{A}]{}(1,1,1,1)^T\oplus\mathbb{R}[\mathscr{A}] {}(0,0,0,1)^TśúÄŚźéŤß£ťáäšłÄšłčťÄČśč©$\alpha_1$ŚíĆ$\alpha_2$ÁöĄŚźąÁźÜśÄß(ŤôĹÁĄ∂ŚģĆŚÖ®ŚŹĮšĽ•ť™ĆŤĮĀ$P^{-1}AP=B$)„ÄāŚ∑≤ÁĽŹÁü•ťĀď,$m_k$šłÄŚģöśėĮ$\alpha_k$ÁöĄśúÄŚįŹťõ∂ŚĆĖŚ≠ź„ÄāŚĮĻšļé$\alpha_2$ÔľĆŤÄÉŤôĎ $ (A-I)\alpha_2=0 $ Ść≥ŚŹĮ„ÄāŤŅôŚąöŚ•ĹŤ¶ĀśĪā$\alpha_2$śėĮ$\mathscr{A}$ŚÖ≥šļéÁČĻŚĺĀŚÄľ$1$šłčÁöĄÁČĻŚĺĀŚźĎťáŹ„ÄāŚĮĻšļé$\alpha_1$ÔľĆŚõ†šłļ$m=\lambda^3+5\lambda^2-\lambda-5$śėĮśúÄŚįŹťõ∂ŚĆĖŚ≠źÔľĆśČÄšĽ•$\alpha_1$ÔľĆ$\mathscr{A}\alpha_1$ÔľĆ$\mathscr{A}^2\alpha_1$ŚŅÖť°ĽÁļŅśÄßśó†ŚÖ≥ÔľĆŚź¶ŚąôŚįĪŤĮīśėéś≠§śó∂$m_1$šłćśėĮ$\alpha_1$ÁöĄśúÄŚįŹťõ∂ŚĆĖŚ≠źÔľĆŤŅôšłé$\mathbb{R}[\mathscr{A}]\alpha_1$ÁöĄśÄßŤī®ÁüõÁõĺ.

posts.title: ŚéčÁľ©ŚģöÁźÜŚíĆŚÖ∂ŚļĒÁĒ®
posts.text: šĽÄšĻąśėĮŚéčÁľ©ŚéčÁľ©(contraction)ÁöĄŚáÜÁ°ģśŹŹŤŅįśėĮŤŅôś†∑ÁöĄÔľö ŚŹĖŚģĆŚ§áŚļ¶ťáŹÁ©ļťóī$X$, Śļ¶ťáŹ$d$, ŚáĹśēį$\varphi$śėĮ$X$Śąįśú¨ŤļęÁöĄśė†ŚįĄ, šłĒśĽ°Ť∂≥ d(\varphi(x),\varphi(y)) \leq cd(x,y). ŚÖ∂šł≠$x,y\in{X}$, $c<1$. ťā£šĻą$\varphi$ŚįĪśėĮšłÄšł™šĽé$X$Śąįśú¨ŤļęÁöĄŚéčÁľ©. šłÄšł™śúÄÁģÄŚćēÁöĄšĺčŚ≠źśėĮ, $y=\frac{x}{2}$. ŤŅôśėĮšłÄšł™‚ÄúŚĻ≥Áľď‚ÄĚÁöĄŚáĹśēį. ŚĻ≥ÁľďÁöĄÁ®čŚļ¶śėĮśÄéś†∑ŚĎĘ? śĖúÁéáŚįŹšļé1. ŤÄĆŚéčÁľ©ŚģöÁźÜśĆáŚáļ, ŚĮĻšļéŤŅôšł™ŚáĹśēį, śúČšłĒšĽÖśúČšłÄšł™$x$šĹŅŚĺó$\varphi(x)=x$. šĺčŚ¶āšłäťĚĘÁöĄšĺčŚ≠ź, ŚŹ™śúČ$0=0$ŤŅôšłÄšł™ÁāĻ. śé•šłčśĚ•Ť¶ĀÁĽôŚáļŤĮĀśėé. ŚéčÁľ©ÁöĄŤĮĀśėéšĽĽŚŹĖ$x_0\in{X}$, śĆČÁÖßŚ¶āšłčśĖĻś≥ēŚģöšĻČśēįŚąó$x_n$: x_{n+1}=\varphi(x_n)šĽéŤÄĆšľöśúČ d(x_2,x_1)=d(\varphi(x_1),\varphi(x_0))\leq cd(x_1,x_0)$$ (Ś¶āśěúšĹ†šłćÁźÜŤß£ŤŅôťáĆÁöĄ$d(x,y)$, ŚŹĮšĽ•ÁģÄŚćēŚúįÁúčśąź$|x-y|$) śé®ŚĻŅšłčŚéĽ, ŚįĪśúČ $$d(x_{n+1},x_n)\leq c^nd(x_1,x_0)ŚĮĻšļé$n<m$, ŚįĪśúČ \begin{equation} \begin{aligned} d(x_n,x_m)\leq\sum_{i=n+1}^md(x_i,x_{i+1})\leq(c^n+c^{n+1}+\cdots+c^{m-1})d(x_1,x_0)\leq\frac{c^n}{1-c}d(x_1,x_0)\end{aligned} \end{equation} ŤŅôŤĮīśėé, $x_n$śėĮšłÄšł™CauchyśēįŚąó, ŚŹąŤÄÉŤôĎŚąį$X$śėĮŚģĆŚ§áÁ©ļťóī, Śõ†ś≠§$x_n$śĒ∂śēõ.Ťģĺ$x_n$ÁöĄśěĀťôźšłļ$x$. ÁĒĪŚéčÁľ©ÁöĄŚģöšĻČŚŹĮÁü•, $\varphi$šłļšłÄŤáīŤŅěÁĽ≠ÁöĄŚáĹśēį, šĽéŤÄĆśúČ \varphi(x)=\lim\limits_{n\to\infty}\varphi(x_n)=\lim\limits_{n\to\infty}x_{n+1}=xŤá≥šļéŚĒĮšłÄśÄßÁöĄŤĮĀśėé, ŚŹĮŤģĺ$\varphi(x)=x$, $\varphi(y)=y$.šĽéŤÄĆśúČ$d(\varphi(x),\varphi(y))=d(x,y)\leq cd(x,y)$ ŤŅôŚŹ™śúČŚú®$d(x,y)=0$śó∂śąźÁęč. ŚĒĮšłÄśÄßŚĺóŚąįŤĮĀśėé. ÁģÄŚćēŚļĒÁĒ®šłčťĚĘśėĮšłÄťĀďśēįŚ≠¶šłďšłöÁ†ĒÁ©∂ÁĒüŚÖ•Ś≠¶ŤÄÉŤĮēťĘė. Śú®ŤŅôšł™ťĘėšł≠ŚŹĮšĽ•ŚŹĎÁéį, Śú®śĆáŚģöÁöĄśĚ°šĽ∂šłč, Śą©ÁĒ®‚ÄúŚéčÁľ©‚ÄĚŤŅôšł™Ś∑•ŚÖ∑, ťóģťĘėÁöĄŤß£ŚÜ≥ŚŹėŚĺóťĚěŚłłÁģÄŚćē. Ś∑≤Áü•$0\leq a\leq 1, b \geq2$, śúČśēįŚąó${x_n}$ śĽ°Ť∂≥$x_0=0$,šłĒśúČťÄíśé®ŚÖ≥Á≥Ľx_{n+1}=x_n-\frac{1}{b}(x_n^2-a),śĪāŤĮĀś≠§śēįŚąóśĒ∂śēõ, ŚĻ∂śĪāŚáļśěĀťôź. Śú®ŤŅôťáĆŤģĺ$\phi(x)=x-\frac{1}{b}(x^2-a)$, ťā£šĻąśúČ$x_{n+1}=\varphi(x_n)$.Ś¶āśěúŤÉĹŤĮĀŚáļ$\varphi$šłļšłÄšł™ŚéčÁľ©, śěĀťôźÁöĄťóģťĘėŚįĪŤŅéŚąÉŤÄĆŤß£šļÜ. ŤĮĀśėé Ťģĺ$\varphi(x)=x-\frac{1}{b}(x^2-a)$. Śąô$x_{n+1}=\varphi(x_n)$.Śú®ŤŅôťáĆ$x_1=\varphi(x_0)=\frac{a}{b}$. ÁéįŤĮĀśėéŚĮĻśČÄśúČ$n>0$ śúČ$\frac{a}{b}\leq x_n\leq 1$,ŚĮĻ$n$ŤŅõŤ°ĆŚĹíÁļ≥. $n=1$śó∂, šłćÁ≠ČŚľŹŚ∑≤śąźÁęč. ŚĀáŤģĺ$n=k$śó∂śąźÁęč, Śąô$n=k+1$śó∂, śúČ x_{k+1}=\frac{a}{b}-\frac{bx_k-x_k^2}{b}ŤÄÉŤôĎŚąį$y=bx-x^2$Śú®$[\frac{a}{b},\frac{b}{2}]$ŚćēŤįÉťÄíŚáŹ, ŤÄĆ$\frac{b}{2}\geq1$, śēÖśúČ x_{n+1}\leq\frac{a}{b} +\frac{b-1}{b}\leq 1 ŤÄĆ$y(\frac{a}{b})\geq y(0)=0$,śēÖ$x_{n+1}\geq\frac{a}{b}$. Śõ†ś≠§šłćÁ≠ČŚľŹŚĮĻśČÄśúČ$n>0$śąźÁęč. Śą©ÁĒ®ŤŅôšł™šłćÁ≠ČŚľŹ, ŚĺóŚąį |f(x)-f(y)|=|x-y||1-\frac{x+y}{b}|\leq|x-y||1-\frac{2a}{b^2}|, \quad \frac{a}{b}\leq x,y \leq 1 $a=0$śó∂, ŚĺóŚąį$x_1=x_2=\cdots=0$, śēÖśěĀťôźŚ≠ėŚú®šłĒšłļ$0$. $a\neq 0$śó∂, šĽ§$c=|1-\frac{2a}{b^2}|$, ŚąôŚßčÁĽąśúČ$c<1$. ś≠§śó∂ŚįĪśėĮšłäśĖášł≠śČÄśŹźŚąįÁöĄŚéčÁľ©. śĒ∂śēõÁöĄŤĮĀśėéÁē•ŚéĽ. Ťģĺ$\lim\limits_{n\to\infty}x_n=x$, Śąô$x=\varphi(x)$. Ťß£Śĺó$x=\sqrt{a}$.ŤŅôŚú®$a=0$śó∂šĻüśąźÁęč. ÁĽľšłä, $\lim\limits_{n\to\infty}x_n$Ś≠ėŚú®, šłĒŚÄľšłļ$\sqrt{a}$. śÄĽÁĽďŚéčÁľ©śė†ŚįĄŚú®Ś§ĄÁźÜŤŅ≠šĽ£ÁĒüśąźÁöĄśēįŚąóśó∂ŚĺąśúČśēą, ŤÄĆšłĒŚŹĮšĽ•śé®ŚĻŅŚąįŚ§öÁĽīÁĒöŤá≥śó†Á©∑ÁĽīšł≠. šĹÜśėĮŤ¶Āś≥®śĄŹ, ŤŅôšł™śė†ŚįĄŚģěŤī®šłäśėĮŚú®Ś§ĄÁźÜŚĪÄťÉ®śÄßŤī®, Ś§ßŤĆÉŚõīÁöĄťĚěÁļŅśÄßÁöĄťóģťĘėŚąôšłćŤÉĹŚ§ĄÁźÜ.Ś§ĄÁźÜŤŅ≠šĽ£śēįŚąóśėĮŚģÉŚú®Á¶Ľśē£ŚĹĘŚľŹšłčÁöĄŚüļśú¨ŚļĒÁĒ®, šĹÜŚéčÁľ©Śú®ŤŅěÁĽ≠ŚĹĘŚľŹšłčšĻüśúČŚĺąŚ§öťáćŤ¶ĀÁöĄŚļĒÁĒ®, šĺčŚ¶āŚú®$\mathbb{R}^n$šł≠ŤĮĀśėéťöźŚáĹśēįŚģöÁźÜ ŚÖ≥šļéŚļ¶ťáŹŚļ¶ťáŹŚáĹśēį$d(x,y)$Ść≥Ť°®Á§ļšł§ÁāĻšĻčťóīÁöĄŤ∑ĚÁ¶Ľ, śĽ°Ť∂≥šĽ•šłčšłČšł™śĚ°šĽ∂Ôľö $d(x,y)\geq 0$, Á≠ČŚŹ∑śąźÁęčŚĹďšłĒšĽÖŚĹď$x=y$. $d(x,y)=d(y,x)$. $d(x,y) + d(y,z) \leq d(x,z)$ (šłČŤßíŚĹĘšłćÁ≠ČŚľŹ) ŤÄĆŚļ¶ťáŹÁ©ļťóīŚć≥šłÄšł™śúČŚļ¶ťáŹŚáĹśēįÁöĄťõÜŚźą. Śļ¶ťáŹÁ©ļťóīÁöĄŚģĆŚ§áśÄßśĆá, ŤŅôšł™Á©ļťóīÁöĄšĽĽśĄŹCauchyŚąóťÉĹśĒ∂śēõ. šłÄšł™ÁģÄŚćēÁöĄšĺčŚ≠źśėĮ, ŚÖ®šĹďŚģěśēį$\mathbb{R}$, Śļ¶ťáŹŚáĹśēį$d(x,y)=|x-y|$. ŤÄĆ$\mathbb{Q}$Śú®ŤŅôšł™ÁõłŚźĆÁöĄŚļ¶ťáŹšłčśėĮŚŹĎśē£ÁöĄ. šĺčŚ¶āCauchyśēįŚąó$\{\left(1+\frac{1}{n}\right)^n\}$ŤŅôšł™śēįŚąóśĒ∂śēõŚąį$e$, ŤÄĆ$e\notin{\mathbb{Q}}$.

posts.title: ÁļŅśÄßÁ©ļťóīÁöĄŚĺ™ÁéĮŚ≠źÁ©ļťóīšłéÁģÄŚćēŚļĒÁĒ®
posts.text: Áü©ťėĶÁöĄśúČÁźÜś†áŚáÜŚĹĘÁü©ťėĶŚĮĻŤßíŚĆĖŚąÜŤß£ŤôĹÁĄ∂šľöŚĺóŚąįśúÄÁģÄŚćēÁöĄŚĹĘŚľŹÔľĆšĹÜśėĮśĚ°šĽ∂śėĮŚĺąŤčõŚąĽÁöĄ„ÄāŚģÉŤ¶ĀśĪāŤĘęŚąÜŤß£ÁöĄ$n$ťė∂Áü©ťėĶśúČ$n$šł™ÁļŅśÄßšłćÁõłŚÖ≥ÁöĄÁČĻŚĺĀŚźĎťáŹÔľĆŚÖ∑šĹďŚúįŤĮīÔľĆšłÄšł™Áü©ťėĶŚŹĮŚĮĻŤßíŚĆĖŚĹďšłĒšĽÖŚĹďŚĮĻŚļĒÁČĻŚĺĀŚÄľÁöĄŚá†šĹēťáćśēįÁ≠ČšļéŚĮĻŚļĒÁöĄšĽ£śēįťáćśēį„ÄāŤŅėśúČŚŹĮŤÉĹťúÄŤ¶ĀśČ©Śľ†śēįŚüüÔľĆšĺčŚ¶āŚģěÁü©ťėĶŤß£ŚáļŚ§ćś†ĻÔľĆŤÄÉŤôĎŚ§ćÁČĻŚĺĀŚźĎťáŹ„ÄāśČÄšĽ•ŤĮīŤŅôÁßćŚąÜŤß£šłćÁ®≥Śģö„ÄāŤÄĆšłĒŚģÉŚĻ∂šłćŤÉĹÁõīśé•ŚŹćśė†šłÄšł™Áü©ťėĶÁöĄśČÄśúČśÄßŤī®„ÄāŚĻłŤŅźÁöĄśėĮÔľĆťô§šļÜŚĮĻŤßíŚĆĖŚąÜŤß£šĻčŚ§ĖŤŅėśúČšłÄšļõšłćŚźĆÁöĄÁü©ťėĶŚąÜŤß£Śäěś≥ē„ÄāŤôĹÁĄ∂ŚĹĘŚľŹšłäšłćŚ¶āŚĮĻŤßíÁü©ťėĶšłÄś†∑ÁģÄśīĀÔľĆšĹÜśėĮŚćīŤÉĹšĹďÁéįšłćŚźĆÁöĄśÄßŤī®‚ÄĒ‚ÄĒšĺčŚ¶āŚĮĻÁČĻŚĺĀŚ§öť°ĻŚľŹÁöĄŚąÜŤß£„ÄāŤÄĆšłĒÔľĆŤŅôšļõŚąÜŤß£Śäěś≥ēŚĮĻšĽĽšĹēÁü©ťėĶťÉĹśėĮŚŹĮŤ°ĆÁöĄ„Äā ť¶ĖŚÖąŤ¶ĀšĽčÁĽćÁöĄśėĮśúČÁźÜś†áŚáÜŚĹĘ(rational form)„ÄāŤŅôťáĆÁöĄ‚ÄúśúČÁźÜ‚ÄĚŚíĆ$\mathbb{Q}$ś≤°śúČŚÖ≥Á≥Ľ„ÄāśČÄŤįď‚Äúrational‚ÄĚśėĮŚõ†šłļÔľĆšłćŚźĆšļéŚÖ∂šĽĖŚá†ÁßćŚąÜŤß£śĖĻŚľŹÔľĆŤŅôÁßćŚąÜŤß£šłćťúÄŤ¶ĀśČ©Śľ†śēįŚüü„ÄāŚÖ∑šĹďśĚ•ŤĮīÔľĆŚĮĻŚüü$\mathbb{F}$šłäšĽĽśĄŹśĖĻťėĶ$A$ÔľĆŚ≠ėŚú®$\mathbb{F}$šłäÁöĄŚŹĮťÄÜÁü©ťėĶ$P$šĹŅŚĺó B=P^{-1}AP=\text{diag}(C(m_1),\cdots,C(m_r)) ŚÖ∂šł≠ÔľĆ$C(m_i)$śėĮ$m_i\in\mathbb{F}(\lambda)$ÁöĄŚŹčťėĶ(companion matrix)ÔľĆśėĮÁĒĪ$\mathbb{F}$šłäÁöĄŚ§öť°ĻŚľŹ$m_i$ŚĒĮšłÄŚÜ≥ŚģöÁöĄ„ÄāŤŅôšļõŚ§öť°ĻŚľŹšĻüśėĮÁĒĪ$A$ŚĒĮšłÄŚÜ≥ŚģöÁöĄÔľĆŚŹęŚĀöÁü©ťėĶÁöĄšłćŚŹėŚõ†Ś≠ź„Äā ŤŅôšļõŚ§öť°ĻŚľŹšĻüśúČÁČĻśģäÁöĄśÄßŤī®(ŤŅôšĻüśėĮŤŅôÁßćŚąÜŤß£ÁöĄśĄŹšĻČśČÄŚú®)„Äāť¶ĖŚÖąÔľĆ$m_1$śėĮ$A$ÁöĄśúÄŚįŹŚ§öť°ĻŚľŹÔľĆ$A$ÁöĄÁČĻŚĺĀŚ§öť°ĻŚľŹŚŹĮšĽ•Ť°®Á§ļšłļ$f(\lambda)=m_1\cdots{m_r}$„ÄāŚú®ť°ļŚļŹšłäÔľĆŚļĒŤĮ•śúČ$m_i|m_{i-1}(i=2,3,\cdotsÔľĆr)$„ÄāšĻüŚįĪśėĮŤĮīś¨°śēįŚ§ßÁöĄŚú®ŚČćťĚĘŚįŹÁöĄŚú®ŚźéťĚĘ„Äā šłÄšł™Ś§öť°ĻŚľŹÁöĄŚŹčťėĶśėĮśĆáŤŅôś†∑ÁöĄšłÄšł™śĖĻťėĶÔľö C(c_0+c_1t+\cdots+c_{n-1}t^{n-1}+t^n)=\begin{pmatrix} 0&0&\cdots&0&-c_0 \\ 1&0&\cdots&0&-c_1 \\ 0&1&\cdots&0&-c_2 \\ \vdots&\vdots&\ddots&\vdots&\vdots \\ 0&0&\cdots&1&-c_{n-1} \end{pmatrix}šłćťöĺŚŹĎÁéįÔľĆšłÄšł™Ś§öť°ĻŚľŹÁöĄśĖĻťėĶśėĮŤĘęŤŅôšłÄšł™śĖĻťėĶŚĒĮšłÄŚÜ≥ŚģöÁöĄ„ÄāŤģ®ŤģļŤŅôšł™Áü©ťėĶÁöĄÁČĻŚĺĀŚ§öť°ĻŚľŹŚŹĮšĽ•ŚŹĎÁéįÁČĻŚĺĀŚ§öť°ĻŚľŹ$f$Ść≥šłļś≠§Ś§öť°ĻŚľŹ„Äā ŤŅôÁĮáŚćöŚģĘťáĆšľöŚÖąšĽčÁĽćšłÄšłčŤŅôÁßćŚąÜŤß£ÁöĄŤÉĆśôĮŚíĆšłÄšļõÁõłŚĮĻÁ≤óÁē•ÁģÄŚćēÁöĄŚąÜŤß£Śģěšĺč„ÄāÁü©ťėĶśúČÁźÜś†áŚáÜŚĹĘÁöĄŚģĆśēīŤģļŤŅįšľöśĒĺŚąįśé•šłčśĚ•ÁöĄŚćöŚģĘšł≠„Äā Śĺ™ÁéĮÁ©ļťóīťúÄŤ¶ĀśĆáŚáļÔľĆŤŅôś†∑šłÄšł™ś†áŚáÜŚěčś∂ČŚŹäŚąįÁļŅśÄßÁ©ļťóīÁöĄŚĺ™ÁéĮŚąÜŤß£ÔľĆŤÄĆŚĺ™ÁéĮŚąÜŤß£ŚŹąś∂ČŚŹäŚąįŚĺ™ÁéĮÁ©ļťóīŤŅôšłÄś¶āŚŅĶ„Äā‚ÄúŚĺ™ÁéĮ‚ÄĚŤŅôšłÄś¶āŚŅĶšĹďÁéįŚú®ŤĘęŚźĆšłÄšł™ÁļŅśÄßŚŹėśćĘŚŹćŚ§ćšĹúÁĒ®„ÄāŚĺ™ÁéĮÁ©ļťóīśĆáÁöĄśėĮŚĮĻśüźšłÄÁļŅśÄßŚŹėśćĘÔľĆśüźšłÄŚźĎťáŹÁöĄśúÄŚįŹšłćŚŹėŚ≠źÁ©ļťóī„Äā Ťģĺ$V$śėĮśēįŚüü$\mathbb{F}$šłäÁöĄÁļŅśÄßÁ©ļťóīÔľĆŚĮĻšļéŚõļŚģöÁöĄÁļŅśÄßŚŹėśćĘ$\mathscr{A}$ÔľĆŚíĆšłÄšł™śĆáŚģöÁöĄŚźĎťáŹ$\alpha \in V$ÔľĆŚļĒŤĮ•śúČ \mathscr{A}\alpha, \mathscr{A}^2\alpha, \cdots\in{V} ŤÄÉŤôĎŚąįÁļŅśÄßÁ©ļťóīŚĮĻŚźĎťáŹÁöĄŚä†ś≥ēŚíĆśēįšĻėÁöĄŚįĀťó≠śÄßÔľĆ$\mathbb{F}$šłäÁöĄŚÖ®šĹďŚ§öť°ĻŚľŹŚįĪśĀįŚ•ĹŚõļŚģöšļÜŤŅôšļõŚźĎťáŹ„ÄāšĻüŚįĪśėĮŤĮīÔľĆŚĮĻšļéśúÄŚįŹšłćŚŹėŚ≠źÁ©ļťóī$W$ÔľĆŚļĒŤĮ•ŚĆÖŚźę$\mathbb{F}$šłäŚÖ®šĹďŚ§öť°ĻŚľŹ$\mathbb{F}[\lambda]$ÁöĄŚÉŹ \mathbb{F}[{\lambda}]{\alpha}=\mathbb{F}[\mathscr{A}]\alpha=\{g(\mathscr{A})\alpha :g(\lambda)\in\mathbb{F}[\lambda]\} ŚŹ¶šłÄśĖĻťĚĘÔľĆ$\mathbb{F}[\lambda]\alpha$Ś∑≤ÁĽŹśėĮšłćŚŹėŚ≠źÁ©ļťóīÔľĆŚõ†ś≠§śúČ$W=\mathbb{F}[\lambda]\alpha$„Äā$W$Áßįšłļ$\alpha$ÁĒüśąź$\mathscr{A}$ÁöĄŚĺ™ÁéĮŚ≠źÁ©ļťóīÔľĆŚ¶āśěúśúČ$W=V$ÔľĆťā£šĻą$V$ÁßįšłļŚĺ™ÁéĮÁ©ļťóīÔľĆ$\alpha$Áßįšłļ$V$ÁöĄŚĺ™ÁéĮŚźĎťáŹ„Äā ťõ∂ŚĆĖŚ≠źŚú®śé•Ťß¶Śĺ™ÁéĮŚąÜŤß£šĻčŚČćÔľĆŚÖąťúÄŤ¶ĀŤÄÉŚĮüšłÄšłčŚĺ™ÁéĮÁ©ļťóīÁöĄ‚ÄúŚ§ßŚįŹ‚ÄĚ„ÄāšłÄšļõÁļŅśÄßÁ©ļťóīÁöĄŚüļśú¨śÄßŤī®Ť¶ĀŚú®ŤŅôťáĆśźěśėéÁôĹ„ÄāŤŅôś†∑šłÄšł™ÁļŅśÄßÁ©ļťóīÁöĄÁĽīśēįśėĮŚ§öŚįĎ? śÄéś†∑ŤÉĹśČĺŚąįŤŅôšł™ÁļŅśÄßÁ©ļťóīÁöĄťõ∂ŚźĎťáŹ? ÁļŅśÄßÁ©ļťóīÁöĄŚüļśÄéšĻąśČĺŚąį? śóĘÁĄ∂śėĮšłćŚŹėŚ≠źÁ©ļťóīÔľĆťā£šĻąťôźŚą∂ÁļŅśÄßŚŹėśćĘÁöĄśĖĻťėĶŤ°®Á§ļŚŹąśėĮśÄéś†∑? ŤŅôšļõťóģťĘėťÉĹťúÄŤ¶ĀťÄöŤŅáŚąÜśěźťõ∂ŚĆĖŚ≠źśĚ•Ťß£ŚÜ≥„Äā Ś¶āśěúŚĮĻŚ§öť°ĻŚľŹ$g(\lambda)$śúČ g(\lambda)\alpha=g(\mathscr{A})\alpha=0 ťā£šĻą$g(\lambda)$Áßįšłļ$\alpha$ÁöĄťõ∂ŚĆĖŚ≠ź(aka ťõ∂ŚĆĖŚ§öť°ĻŚľŹ)„Äā $\alpha$ÁöĄś¨°śēįśúÄšĹéÁöĄť¶ĖšłÄťõ∂ŚĆĖŚ§öť°ĻŚľŹÁßįšłļśúÄŚįŹťõ∂ŚĆĖŚ≠ź„ÄāśėĺÁĄ∂ÔľĆťõ∂ŚĆĖŚ≠źśėĮśúÄŚįŹťõ∂ŚĆĖŚ≠źÁöĄŚÄć„Äāś≥®śĄŹŤŅôťáĆÁöĄťõ∂ŚĆĖŚ≠źśėĮÁõłŚĮĻšļé$\alpha$ÔľĆŤŅôŚíĆÁļŅśÄßŚŹėśćĘÁöĄťõ∂ŚĆĖŚ≠źśėĮšł§Śõěšļč„Äāśé•šłčśĚ•šľöťÄöŤŅáŚąÜśěźÁļŅśÄßÁõłŚÖ≥śÄßśČĺŚáļśúÄŚįŹťõ∂ŚĆĖŚ≠ź„Äā Ś¶āšĹēśČĺŚąįśúÄŚįŹťõ∂ŚĆĖŚ≠źťÄČŚŹĖŚĺ™ÁéĮŚźĎťáŹ$\alpha\in{W}$ÔľĆŚÜćŤÄÉŚĮüŚźĎťáŹ$\mathscr{A}\alpha$śėĮŚź¶ŚíĆ$\alpha$ÁļŅśÄßÁõłŚÖ≥ÔľĆŚ¶āśěúŚź¶ÔľĆšĻüŚįÜ$\mathscr{A}\alpha$ťÄČŚŹĖÔľĆŚÜćśĆČÁÖßŚźĆś†∑ÁöĄŚäěś≥ēťÄČŚŹĖ$\mathscr{A}^2\alpha,\cdots,\mathscr{A}^{k-1}\alpha$ÔľĆŤÄĆ$\alpha,\mathscr{A}\alpha,\mathscr{A}^2\alpha,\cdots,\mathscr{A}^{k-1}\alpha,\mathscr{A}^k\alpha$ÁļŅśÄßÁõłŚÖ≥„Äāś†ĻśćģÁļŅśÄßÁ©ļťóīÁöĄśÄßŤī®ÔľĆ$\mathscr{A}^k\alpha$ŚŹĮšĽ•ŤĘęŚČćťĚĘ$(k-1)$šł™ŚźĎťáŹÁļŅśÄßŤ°®Á§ļŚáļśĚ•„ÄāšĻüŚįĪśėĮŤĮīśúČ$c_0,\cdots,c_{k-1}\in\mathbb{F}$šĹŅŚĺó \mathscr{A}^{k}\alpha+c_{k-1}\mathscr{A}^{k-1}\alpha+\cdots+c_1\mathscr{A}\alpha+c_0\alpha=0 Ś¶āśěúšĽ§$m_{\alpha}(\lambda)=\lambda^k+c_{k-1}\lambda^{k-1}+\cdots+c_0$ÔľĆťā£šĻąŚįĪśúČ$m_\alpha(\mathscr{A})\alpha=0$„ÄāšĻüŚįĪśėĮŤĮīÔľĆ$m_\alpha$śėĮ$\alpha$ÁöĄťõ∂ŚĆĖŚ≠ź„Äāśé•šłčśĚ•ťúÄŤ¶Āť™ĆŤĮĀŤŅôśėĮś¨°śēįśúÄšĹéÁöĄť¶ĖšłÄŚ§öť°ĻŚľŹ„Äā Ťģĺ$g(\lambda)$šłļ$\alpha$ÁöĄťõ∂ŚĆĖŚ§öť°ĻŚľŹ„Äāťā£šĻą$g(\lambda)$ŚŹĮšĽ•ŚÜôśąź g(\lambda)=m_{\alpha}(\lambda)q(\lambda)+r(\lambda) posts.title: Properties of Cyclotomic Polynomials
posts.text:
Background in Basic Field TheoryLet $K$ be a field (in this post we mostly assume that $K \supset \mathbb{Q}$) and $n$ an integer $>1$ which is not divisible by the characteristic of $K$. Then the polynomial X^n-1is separable because its derivative is $nX^{n-1} 
e 0$. Hence in the algebraic closure $\overline{K}$, the polynomial has $n$ distinct roots, which forms a group $U$, and is cyclic. In fact, as an exercise, one can show that, for a field $k$, any subgroup $U$ of the multiplicative group $k^\ast$ is a cyclic group. The generator $\zeta_n$ of $U$ is called the primitive $n$-th root of unity. Let $K=\mathbb{Q}$ and $L$ be the smallest extension that contains all elements of $U$, then we have $L=\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_n)$. As a matter of fact, $L/K$ is a Galois extension (to be shown later), and the cyclotomic polynomial $\Phi_n(X)$ is the irreducible polynomial of $\zeta_n$ over $\mathbb{Q}$. We first need to find the degree $[L:K]$. Proposition 1. Notation being above, $L/K$ is Galois, the Galois group $\operatorname{Gal}(L/K) \cong (\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^\ast$ (the group of units in $\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}$) and $[L:K]=\varphi(n)$. Let‚Äôs first elaborate the fact that $|(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^\ast|=\varphi(n)$. Let $[0],[1],\dots,[n-1]$ be representatives of $\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}$. An element $[x]$ in $\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}$ is a unit if and only if there exists $[y]$ such that $[xy]=[1]$, which is to say, $xy \equiv 1 \mod n$. Notice that $xy \equiv 1 \mod n$ if and only if $xy+mn=1$ for some $y,n \in \mathbb{Z}$, if and only if $\gcd(x,n)=1$. Therefore $|(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^\ast|=\varphi(n)$ is proved. The proof can be produced by two lemmas, the first of which is independent to the characteristic of the field. Lemma 1. Let $k$ be a field and $n$ be not divisible by the characteristic $p$. Let $\zeta=\zeta_n$ be a primitive $n$-th root of unity in $\overline{k}$, then $(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^\ast \supset \operatorname{Gal}(k(\zeta)/k)$ and therefore $[k(\zeta):k] \le \varphi(n)$. Besides, $k(\zeta)/k$ is a normal abelian extension. Proof. Let $\sigma$ be an embedding of $k(\zeta)$ in $\overline{k}$ over $k$, then (\sigma\zeta)^n=\sigma(\zeta^n)=\sigma(1)=1so that $\sigma\zeta$ is also an $n$-th root of unity also. Hence $\sigma\zeta=\zeta^i$ for some $i=i(\sigma)$, uniquely determined modulo $n$. It follows that $\sigma$ maps $k(\zeta)$ into itself. This is to say, $k(\zeta)$ is normal over $k$. Let $	au$ be another automorphism of $k(\zeta)$ over $k$ then \sigma	au\zeta=\zeta^{i(\sigma)i(	au)}.It follows that $i(\sigma)$ and $i(	au)$ are prime to $n$ (otherwise, $\sigma\zeta$ would have a period smaller than $n$, implying that the period of $\zeta$ is smaller than $n$, which is absurd). Therefore for each $\sigma \in \operatorname{Gal}(k(\zeta)/k)$, $i(\sigma)$ can be embedded into $(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^\ast$, thus proving our theorem. $\square$ It is easy to find an example with strict inclusion. One only needs to look at $k=\mathbb{R}$ or $\mathbb{C}$. Lemma 2. Let $\zeta=\zeta_n$ be a primitive $n$-th root of polynomial over $\mathbb{Q}$, then for any $p 
mid n$, $\zeta^p$ is also a primitive $n$-th root of unity. Proof. Let $f(X)$ be the irreducible polynomial of $\zeta$ over $\mathbb{Q}$, then $f(X)|(X^n-1)$ by definition. As a result we can write $X^n-1=f(X)h(X)$ where $h(X)$ has leading coefficient $1$. By Gauss‚Äôs lemma, both $f$ and $h$ have integral coefficients. Suppose $\zeta^p$ is not a root of $f$. Since $(\zeta^p)^n-1=(\zeta^n)^p-1=0$, it follows that $\zeta^p$ is a root of $h$, and $\zeta$ is a root of $h(X^p)$. As a result, $f(X)$ divides $h(X^p)$ and we write h(X^p)=f(X)g(X).Again by Gauss‚Äôs lemma, $g(X)$ has integral coefficients. Next we reduce these equations in $\mathbf{F}_p=\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}$. We firstly have \overline{f}(X)\overline{g}(X)=\overline{h}(X^p).By Fermat‚Äôs little theorem $a^p=a$ for all $a \in \mathbf{F}_q$, we also have \overline{h}(X^p)=\overline{h}(X)^p.Therefore \overline{f}(X)\overline{g}(X)=\overline{h}(X)^p,which implies that $\overline{f}(X)|\overline{h}(X)^p$. Hence $\overline{f}$ and $\overline{h}$ must have a common factor. As a result, $X^n-\overline{1}=\overline{f}(X)\overline{h}(X)$ has multiple roots in $\mathbf{F}_p$, which is impossible because of our choice of $p$. $\square$ Now we are ready for Proposition 1. Proof of Proposition 1. Since $\mathbb{Q}$ is a perfect field, $\mathbb{Q}(\zeta)/\mathbb{Q}$ is automatically separable. This extension is Galois because of lemma 1. By lemma 1, it suffices to show that $[\mathbb{Q}(\zeta):\mathbb{Q}] \ge \varphi(n)$. Recall in elementary group theory, if $G$ is a finite cyclic group of order $m$ and $x$ is a generator of $G$, then the set of generators consists elements of the form $x^
u$ where $
u 
mid m$. In this occasion, if $\zeta$ generates $U$, then $\zeta^p$ also generates $U$ because $p 
mid n$. It follows that every primitive $n$-th root of unity can be obtained by raising $\zeta$ to a succession of prime numbers that do not divide $n$ (as a result we obtain exactly $\varphi(n)$ such primitive roots). By lemma 2, all these numbers are roots of $f$ in the proof of lemma 2. Therefore $\deg f = [L:K] \ge \varphi(n)$. Hence the proposition is proved. $\square$ We will show that $f$ in the proof lemma 2 is actually the cyclotomic polynomial $\Phi_n(x)$ you are looking for. The following procedure works for all fields where the characteristic does not divide $n$, but we assume characteristic to be $0$ for simplicity. We have X^n-1=\prod_{\zeta}(X-\zeta),where the product is taken over all $n$-th roots of unity. Collecting all roots with the same period $d$ (i.e., those $\zeta$ such that $\zeta^d=1$), we put \Phi_d(X)=\prod_{\operatorname{period} \zeta=d}(X-\zeta).Then X^n-1=\prod_{d|n}\Phi_d(X).It follows that $\Phi_1(X)=X-1$ and \Phi_n(X)= rac{X^n-1}{\prod_{d\mid n}^{d

posts.title: Dedekind Domain and Properties in an Elementary Approach
A long exact sequence of cohomology groups (zig-zag and diagram-chasing)
posts.text: Exterior differentiation(This section is intended to introduce the background. Feel free to skip if you already know exterior differentiation.) There are several useful tools for vector calculus on $\mathbb{R}^3,$ namely gradient, curl, and divergence. It is possible to treat the gradient of a differentiable function $f$ on $\mathbb{R}^3$ at a point $x_0$ as the Fr√©chet derivative at $x_0$. But it does not work for curl and divergence at all. Fortunately there is another abstraction that works for all of them. It comes from differential forms. Let $x_1,\cdots,x_n$ be the linear coordinates on $\mathbb{R}^n$ as usual. We define an algebra $\Omega^{\ast}$ over $\mathbb{R}$ generated by $dx_1,\cdots,dx_n$ with the following relations: \begin{cases} dx_idx_i=0 \\ dx_idx_j = -dx_jdx_i \quad i \neq j \end{cases}This is a vector space as well, and it‚Äôs easy to derive that it has a basis by 1,dx_i,dx_idx_j,dx_idx_jdx_k,\cdots,dx_1\dots dx_nwhere $i<j<k$. The $C^{\infty}$ differential forms on $\mathbb{R}^n$ are defined to be the tensor product \Omega^*(\mathbb{R}^n)=\{C^{\infty}\text{ functions on }\mathbb{R}^n\} \otimes_\mathbb{R}\Omega^*.As is can be shown, for $\omega \in \Omega^{\ast}(\mathbb{R}^n)$, we have a unique representation by \omega=\sum f_{i_1\cdots i_k}dx_{i_1}\dots dx_{i_k},and in this case we also say $\omega$ is a $C^{\infty}$ $k$-form on $\mathbb{R}^n$ (for simplicity we also write $\omega=\sum f_Idx_I$). The algebra of all $k$-forms will be denoted by $\Omega^k(\mathbb{R}^n)$. And naturally we have $\Omega^{\ast}(\mathbb{R}^n)$ to be graded since \Omega^{*}(\mathbb{R}^n)=\bigoplus_{k=0}^{n}\Omega^k(\mathbb{R}^n).The operator $d$But if we have $\omega \in \Omega^0(\mathbb{R}^n)$, we see $\omega$ is merely a $C^{\infty}$ function. As taught in multivariable calculus course, for the differential of $\omega$ we have d\omega=\sum_{i}\partial\omega/\partial x_idx_iand it turns out that $d\omega\in\Omega^{1}(\mathbb{R}^n)$. This inspires us to obtain a generalization onto the differential operator $d$: \begin{aligned} d:\Omega^{k}(\mathbb{R}^n) &\to \Omega^{k+1}(\mathbb{R}^n) \\ \omega &\mapsto d\omega \end{aligned}and $d\omega$ is defined as follows. The case when $k=0$ is defined as usual (just the one above). For $k>0$ and $\omega=\sum f_I dx_I,$ $d\omega$ is defined ‚Äėinductively‚Äô by d\omega=\sum df_I dx_I.This $d$ is the so-called exterior differentiation, which serves as the ultimate abstract extension of gradient, curl, divergence, etc. If we restrict ourself to $\mathbb{R}^3$, we see these vector calculus tools comes up in the nature of things. Functions df=\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}dx+\frac{\partial f}{\partial y}dy+\frac{\partial f}{\partial z}dz.$1$-forms d(f_1dx+f_2dy+f_3dz)=\left(\frac{\partial f_3}{\partial y}-\frac{\partial f_2}{\partial z}\right)dydz-\left(\frac{\partial f_1}{\partial z}-\frac{\partial f_3}{\partial x}\right)dxdz+\left(\frac{\partial f_2}{\partial x}-\frac{\partial f_1}{\partial y}\right)dxdy.$2$-forms d(f_1dydz-f_2dxdz+f_3dxdy)=\left(\frac{\partial f_1}{\partial x}+\frac{\partial f_2}{\partial y}+ \frac{\partial f_3}{\partial z}\right)dxdydz.The calculation is tedious but a nice exercise to understand the definition of $d$ and $\Omega^{\ast}$. Conservative field - on the kernel and image of $d$By elementary computation we are also able to show that $d^2\omega=0$ for all $\omega \in \Omega^{\ast}(\mathbb{R}^n)$ (Hint: $\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_i \partial x_j}=\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_j \partial x_i}$ but $dx_idx_j=-dx_idx_j$). Now we consider a vector field $\overrightarrow{v}=(v_1,v_2)$ of dimension $2$. If $C$ is an arbitrary simply closed smooth curve in $\mathbb{R}^2$, then we expect \oint_C\overrightarrow{v}d\overrightarrow{r}=\oint_C v_1dx+v_2dyto be $0$. If this happens (note the arbitrary of $C$), we say $\overrightarrow{v}$ to be a conservative field (path independent). So when conservative? It happens when there is a function $f$ such that \nabla f=\overrightarrow{v}=(v_1,v_2)=(\partial{f}/\partial{x},\partial{f}/\partial{y}).This is equivalent to say that df=v_1dx+v_2dy.If we use $C^{\ast}$ to denote the area enclosed by $C$, by Green‚Äôs theorem, we have \begin{aligned} \oint_C v_1dx+v_2dy&=\iint_{C^*}\left(\frac{\partial{v_2}}{\partial{x}}-\frac{\partial{v_1}}{\partial{y}}\right)dxdy \\ &=\iint_{C^*}d(v_1dx+v_2dy) \\ &=\iint_{C^*}d^2f \\ &=0 \end{aligned}If you translate what you‚Äôve learned in multivariable calculus course (path independence) into the language of differential form, you will see that the set of all conservative fields is precisely the image of $d_0:\Omega^0(\mathbb{R}^2) \to \Omega^1(\mathbb{R}^2)$. Also, they are in the kernel of the next $d_1:\Omega^1(\mathbb{R}^2) \to \Omega^2(\mathbb{R}^2)$. These $d$‚Äôs are naturally homomorphism, so it‚Äôs natural to discuss the factor group. But before that, we need some terminologies. de Rham complex and de Rham cohomology groupThe complex $\Omega^{\ast}(\mathbb{R}^n)$ together with $d$ is called the de Rham complex on $\mathbb{R}^n$. Now consider the sequence \Omega^0(\mathbb{R}^n)\xrightarrow{d_0}\Omega^1(\mathbb{R}^n)\xrightarrow{d_1}\cdots\xrightarrow{d_{n-2}}\Omega^{n-1}(\mathbb{R}^n)\xrightarrow{d_{n-1}}\Omega^{n}(\mathbb{R^n}).We say $\omega \in \Omega^k(\mathbb{R}^n)$ is closed if $d_k\omega=0$, or equivalently, $\omega \in \ker d_k$. Dually, we say $\omega$ is exact if there exists some $\mu \in \Omega^{k-1}(\mathbb{R}^n)$ such that $d\mu=\omega$, that is, $\omega \in \operatorname{im}d_{k-1}$. Of course all $d_k$‚Äôs can be written as $d$ but the index makes it easier to understand. Instead of doing integration or differentiation, which is ‚Äėuninteresting‚Äô, we are going to discuss the abstract structure of it. The $k$-th de Rham cohomology in $\mathbb{R}^n$ is defined to be the factor space H_{DR}^{k}(\mathbb{R}^n)=\frac{\ker d_k}{\operatorname{im} d_{k-1}}.As an example, note that by the fundamental theorem of calculus, every $1$-form is exact, therefore $H_{DR}^1(\mathbb{R})=0$. Since de Rham complex is a special case of differential complex, and other restrictions of de Rham complex plays no critical role thereafter, we are going discuss the algebraic structure of differential complex directly. The long exact sequence of cohomology groupsWe are going to show that, there exists a long exact sequence of cohomology groups after a short exact sequence is defined. For the convenience let‚Äôs recall here some basic definitions Exact sequenceA sequence of vector spaces (or groups) \cdots \rightarrow G_{k-1} \xrightarrow{f_{k-1}} G_k \xrightarrow{f_k} G_{k+1} \xrightarrow{f_{k+1}}\cdotsis said to be exact if the image of $f_{k-1}$ is the kernel of $f_k$ for all $k$. Sometimes we need to discuss a extremely short one by 0 \rightarrow A \xrightarrow{f} B \xrightarrow{g} C \rightarrow 0.As one can see, $f$ is injective and $g$ is surjective. Differential complexA direct sum of vector spaces $C=\oplus_{k \in \mathbb{Z}}C^k$ is called a differential complex if there are homomorphisms by \cdots \rightarrow C^{k-1} \xrightarrow{d_{k-1}} C^k \xrightarrow{d_k} C^{k+1} \xrightarrow{d_{k+1}}\cdotssuch that $d_{k-1}d_k=0$. Sometimes we write $d$ instead of $d_{k}$ since this differential operator of $C$ is universal. Therefore we may also say that $d^2=0$. The cohomology of $C$ is the direct sum of vector spaces $H(C)=\oplus_{k \in \mathbb{Z}}H^k(C) $ where H^k(C)=\frac{\ker d_{k}}{\operatorname{im}d_{k-1}}.A map $f: A \to B$ where $A$ and $B$ are differential complexes, is called a chain map if we have $fd_A=d_Bf$. The sequenceNow consider a short exact sequence of differential complexes 0 \rightarrow A \xrightarrow{f} B \xrightarrow{g} C \rightarrow 0where both $f$ and $g$ are chain maps (this is important). Then there exists a long exact sequence by \cdots\rightarrow H^q(A) \xrightarrow{f^*} H^{q}(B) \xrightarrow{g^*} H^q(C)\xrightarrow{d^{*}}H^{q+1}(A) \xrightarrow{f^*}\cdots.Here, $f^{\ast}$ and $g^{\ast}$ are the naturally induced maps. For $c \in C^q$, $d^{\ast}[c]$ is defined to be the cohomology class $[a]$ where $a \in A^{q+1}$, and that $f(a)=db$, and that $g(b)=c$. The sequence can be described using the two-layer commutative diagram below. The long exact sequence is actually the purple one (you see why people may call this zig-zag lemma). This sequence is ‚Äėbased on‚Äô the blue diagram, which can be considered naturally as an expansion of the short exact sequence. The method that will be used in the following proof is called diagram-chasing, whose importance has already been described by Professor James Munkres: master this. We will be abusing the properties of almost every homomorphism and group appeared in this commutative diagram to trace the elements. ProofFirst, we give a precise definition of $d^{\ast}$. For a closed $c \in C^q$, by the surjectivity of $g$ (note this sequence is exact), there exists some $b \in B^q$ such that $g(b)=c$. But $g(db)=d(g(b))=dc=0$, we see for $db \in B^{q+1}$ we have $db \in \ker g$. By the exactness of the sequence, we see $db \in \operatorname{im}{f}$, that is, there exists some $a \in A^{q+1}$ such that $f(a)=db$. Further, $a$ is closed since f(da)=d(f(a))=d^2b=0and we already know that $f$ has trivial kernel (which contains $da$). $d^{\ast}$ is therefore defined by d^*[c]=[a],where $[\cdot]$ means ‚Äúthe homology class of‚ÄĚ. But it is expected that $d^{\ast}$ is a well-defined homomorphism. Let $c_q$ and $c_q‚Äô$ be two closed forms in $C^q$. To show $d^{\ast}$ is well-defined, we suppose $[c_q]=[c_q‚Äô]$ (i.e. they are homologous). Choose $b_q$ and $b_q‚Äô$ so that $g(b_q)=c_q$ and $g(b_q‚Äô)=c_q‚Äô$. Accordingly, we also pick $a_{q+1}$ and $a_{q+1}‚Äô$ such that $f(a_{q+1})=db_q$ and $f(a_{q+1}‚Äô)=db_q‚Äô$. By definition of $d^{\ast}$, we need to show that $[a_{q+1}]=[a_{q+1}‚Äô]$. Recall the properties of factor group. $[c_q]=[c_q‚Äô]$ if and only if $c_q-c_q‚Äô \in \operatorname{im}d$. Therefore we can pick some $c_{q-1} \in C^{q-1}$ such that $c_q-c_q‚Äô=dc_{q-1}$. Again, by the surjectivity of $g$, there is some $b_{q-1}$ such that $g(b_{q-1})=c_{q-1}$. Note that \begin{aligned} g(b_q-b_q'-db_{q-1})&=c_q-c_{q}'-g(db_{q-1}) \\ &=dc_{q-1}-d(g(b_{q-1})) \\ &=dc_{q-1}-dc_{q-1} \\ &= 0. \end{aligned}Therefore $b_q-b_q‚Äô-db_{q-1} \in \operatorname{im} f$. We are able to pick some $a_q \in A^{q}$ such that $f(a_q)=b_q-b_q‚Äô-db_{q-1}$. But now we have \begin{aligned} f(da_q)=df(a_q)&=d(b_q-b_q'-db_{q-1}) \\ &=db_q-db_q'-d^2b_{q-1} \\ &=db_q-db_q' \\ &=f(a_{q+1}-a_{q+1}'). \end{aligned}Since $f$ is injective, we have $da_q=a_{q+1}-a_{q+1}‚Äô$, which implies that $a_{q+1}-a_{q+1}‚Äô \in \operatorname{im}d$. Hence $[a_{q+1}]=[a_{q+1}‚Äô]$. To show that $d^{\ast}$ is a homomorphism, note that $g(b_q+b_q‚Äô)=c_q+c_q‚Äô$ and $f(a_{q+1}+a_{q+1}‚Äô)=d(b_q+b_q‚Äô)$. Thus we have d^*[c_q+c_q']=[a_{q+1}+a_{q+1}'].The latter equals $[a_{q+1}]+[a_{q+1}‚Äô]$ since the canonical map is a homomorphism. Therefore we have d^*[c_q+c_q']=d^*[c_q]+d^*[c_q'].Therefore the long sequence exists. It remains to prove exactness. Firstly we need to prove exactness at $H^q(B)$. Pick $[b] \in H^q(B)$. If there is some $a \in A^q$ such that $f(a)=b$, then $g(f(a))=0$. Therefore $g^{\ast}[b]=g^{\ast}[f(a)]=[g(f(a))]=[0]$; hence $\operatorname{im}f \subset \ker g$. Conversely, suppose now $g^{\ast}[b]=[0]$, we shall show that there exists some $[a] \in H^q(A)$ such that $f^{\ast}[a]=[b]$. Note $g^{\ast}[b]=\operatorname{im}d$ where $d$ is the differential operator of $C$ (why?). Therefore there exists some $c_{q-1} \in C^{q-1}$ such that $g(b)=dc_{q-1}$. Pick some $b_{q-1}$ such that $g(b_{q-1})=c_{q-1}$. Then we have g(b-db_{q-1})=g(b)-d(g(b_{q-1}))=g(b)-dc_{q-1}=0.Therefore $f(a)=b-db_{q-1}$ for some $a \in A^q$. Note $a$ is closed since f(da)=df(a)=d(b-db_{q-1})=db-d^2b_{q-1}=db=0and $f$ is injective. $db=0$ since we have g(db)=d(g(b))=d(dc_{q-1})=0.Furthermore, f^*[a]=[f(a)]=[b-dc_{q-1}]=[b]-[0]=[b].Therefore $\ker g^{\ast} \subset \operatorname{im} f$ as desired. Now we prove exactness at $H^q(C)$. (Notation:) pick $[c_q] \in H^q(C)$, there exists some $b_q$ such that $g(b_q)=c_q$; choose $a_{q+1}$ such that $f(a_{q+1})=db_q$. Then $d^{\ast}[c_q]=[a_{q+1}]$ by definition. If $[c_q] \in \operatorname{im}g^{\ast}$, we see $[c_q]=[g(b_q)]=g^{\ast}[b_q]$. But $b_q$ is closed since $[b_q] \in H^q(B)$, we see $f(a_{q+1})=db_q=0$, therefore $d^{\ast}[c_q]=[a_{q+1}]=[0]$ since $f$ is injective. Therefore $\operatorname{im}g^{\ast} \subset \ker d^{\ast}$. Conversely, suppose $d^{\ast}[c^q]=[0]$. By definition of $H^{q+1}(A)$, there is some $a_q \in A$ such that $da_q = a_{q+1}$ (can you see why?). We claim that $b_q-f(a_q)$ is closed and we have $[c_q]=g^{\ast}[b_q-f(a_q)]$. By direct computation, d(b_q-f(a_q))=db_q-d(f(a_q))=db_q-f(d(a_q))=db_q-f(a_{q+1})=0.Meanwhile g^*[b_q-f(a_q)]=[g(b_q)]-[g(f(a_q))]=[c_q].Therefore $\ker d^{\ast} \subset \operatorname{im}g^{\ast}$. Note that $g(f(a_q))=0$ by exactness. Finally, we prove exactness at $H^{q+1}(A)$. Pick $\alpha \in H^{q+1}(A)$. If $\alpha \in \operatorname{im}d^{\ast}$, then $\alpha=[a_{q+1}]$ where $f(a_{q+1})=db_q$ by definition. Then f^*(\alpha)=[f(a_{q+1})]=[db_q]=[0].Therefore $\alpha \in \ker f^{\ast}$. Conversely, if we have $f^{\ast}(\alpha)=[0]$, pick the representative element of $\alpha$, namely we write $\alpha=[a]$; then $[f(a)]=[0]$. But this implies that $f(a) \in \operatorname{im}d$ where $d$ denotes the differential operator of $B$. There exists some $b_{q+1} \in B^{q+1}$ and $b_q \in B^q$ such that $db_{q}=b_{q+1}$. Suppose now $c_q=g(b_q)$. $c_q$ is closed since $dc_q=g(db_q)=g(b_{q+1})=g(f(a))=0$. By definition, $\alpha=d^{\ast}[c_q]$. Therefore $\ker f^{\ast} \subset \operatorname{im}d^{\ast}$. RemarksAs you may see, almost every property of the diagram has been used. The exactness at $B^q$ ensures that $g(f(a))=0$. The definition of $H^q(A)$ ensures that we can simplify the meaning of $[0]$. We even use the injectivity of $f$ and the surjectivity of $g$. This proof is also a demonstration of diagram-chasing technique. As you have seen, we keep running through the diagram to ensure that there is ‚Äúsomeone waiting‚ÄĚ at the destination. This long exact group is useful. Here is an example. Application: Mayer-Vietoris SequenceBy differential forms on a open set $U \subset \mathbb{R}^n$, we mean \Omega^*(U)=\{C^{\infty}\text{ functions on }U\}\otimes_\mathbb{R}\Omega^*.And the de Rham cohomology of $U$ comes up in the nature of things. We are able to compute the cohomology of the union of two open sets. Suppose $M=U \cup V$ is a manifold with $U$ and $V$ open, and $U \amalg V$ is the disjoint union of $U$ and $V$ (the coproduct in the category of sets). $\partial_0$ and $\partial_1$ are inclusions of $U \cap V$ in $U$ and $V$ respectively. We have a natural sequence of inclusions M \leftarrow U\amalg V \leftleftarrows^{\partial_0}_{\partial_1}\leftleftarrows U \cap V.Since $\Omega^{*}$ can also be treated as a contravariant functor from the category of Euclidean spaces with smooth maps to the category of commutative differential graded algebras and their homomorphisms, we have \Omega^*(M) \rightarrow \Omega^*(U) \oplus \Omega^*(V) \rightrightarrows^{\partial^*_0}_{\partial^*_1}\rightrightarrows\Omega^*({U \cap V}).By taking the difference of the last two maps, we have \begin{aligned} 0 \rightarrow \Omega^*(M) \rightarrow \Omega^*(U) \oplus \Omega^*(V) &\rightarrow \Omega^*(U \cap V) \rightarrow 0 \\ (\omega,\tau) &\mapsto \tau-\omega \end{aligned}The sequence above is a short exact sequence. Therefore we may use the zig-zag lemma to find a long exact sequence (which is also called the Mayer-Vietoris sequence) by \cdots\to H^q(M) \to H^q(U) \oplus H^q(V) \to H^q(U \cap V) \xrightarrow{d^*} H^{q+1}(M) \to \cdotsAn exampleThis sequence allows one to compute the cohomology of two union of two open sets. For example, for $H^{*}_{DR}(\mathbb{R}^2-P-Q)$, where $P(x_p,y_p)$ and $Q(x_q,y_q)$ are two distinct points in $\mathbb{R}^2$, we may write (\mathbb{R}^2-P)\cap(\mathbb{R}^2-Q)=\mathbb{R}^2-P-Qand (\mathbb{R}^2-P)\cup(\mathbb{R}^2-Q)=\mathbb{R}^2.Therefore we may write $M=\mathbb{R}^2$, $U=\mathbb{R}^2-P$ and $V=\mathbb{R}^2-Q$. For $U$ and $V$, we have another decomposition by \mathbb{R}^2-P=(\mathbb{R}^2-P_x)\cup(\mathbb{R}^2-P_y)where P_x=\{(x,y_p):x \in \mathbb{R}\}.But (\mathbb{R}^2-P_x)\cap(\mathbb{R}^2-P_y)is a four-time (homeomorphic) copy of $\mathbb{R}^2$. So things become clear after we compute $H^{\ast}_{DR}(\mathbb{R}^2)$. References / Further reading Raoul Bott, Loring W. Tu, Differential Forms in Algebraic Topology Munkres J. R., Elements of Algebraic Topology Micheal Spivak, Calculus on Manifolds Serge Lang, Algebra
